element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Foundation mechanic; player begins with empty map and zones land
Required to connect zones and enable population growth

element_introduction_order.element: Power plants (coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Essential for city function; introduces resource management and long-term planning (limited lifespan, rebuild cycles)
Underground layers introduce spatial complexity; failure causes health/population decline

element_introduction_order.element: Public services (schools, libraries, hospitals, police, fire)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Quality-of-life systems; affects population happiness and retention
Tax rates per zone (residential/commercial/industrial); deficit spending causes game over

element_introduction_order.element: Advanced infrastructure (subways, train depots, bus depots, seaports, airports)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Complex multi-tile placement; high cost; enables trade with neighboring cities

element_introduction_order.element: Advanced power plants (wind, hydroelectric, solar, fusion, satellite microwave)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Environmental concerns and futuristic options; specialized placement (hydroelectric requires waterfalls)
Optional high-value developments; arcologies represent advanced city planning
Policy layer affecting city behavior and finances
Highway connections to neighboring cities; economic complexity layer
Random events that damage infrastructure and require response; creates reactive gameplay moments

typical_relief_triggers: Successful infrastructure expansion enabling new zones
typical_tension_triggers: Power plant aging and requiring replacement
Population decline due to poor services
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Long-cycle tension: SimCity 2000 operates on extended time scales (simulated years/decades). Tension builds gradually through infrastructure aging, resource depletion, and budget pressure. Relief comes from successful city growth and milestone achievements. Peaks during infrastructure collapse periods or unpredictable disasters. Rhythm is deliberate and strategic rather than urgent.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Advanced power plants (wind, solar) and specialized facilities (museums, marinas, arcologies) appear as player wealth and city size increase; not required for basic functionality
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Query tool reveals hidden information (power consumption %, traffic levels, land value); special easter egg: querying library displays Neil Gaiman essay

difficulty_curve.early_game: Permissive: Basic zoning and road building with immediate feedback; player can experiment with city layout without severe penalty
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate constraint: Budget discipline becomes necessary; multiple infrastructure systems requiring balanced investment; service coverage affects population stability
difficulty_curve.late_game: High complexity: Cascading infrastructure aging creates synchronized replacement crises; advanced facilities have steep costs; environmental/economic balance becomes delicate; inter-city trade adds strategic dimension
notable_spikes: First budget deficit crossover (teaches financial consequences)
Simultaneous aging of early power plants (infrastructure collapse)
Large-scale disaster followed by rebuilding demand
Transition from growth to maintenance (shift in player mindset)

notable_level_patterns.description: No discrete levels. Player controls pacing entirely. Game provides newspapers with variable information (random titles, humorous headlines, warnings) that signal state changes and create narrative backdrop to city evolution

Power plants have limited lifespan and must be rebuilt periodically. This creates recurring decision points and forces planning beyond immediate expansion. Different plant types have different lifespans and economics

Introduction of elevation and underground layers (water pipes, subways, road tunnels) adds spatial complexity while maintaining clarity. Near-isometric dimetric view enables clear visualization of multi-level infrastructure

Budget deficit spending leads to game over, but penalty is delayed; population decline from poor service happens over simulated time. Creates tension arc as warning signs accumulate (newspaper articles, opinion polls, darkened abandoned buildings)
